meta.title: Kotlin Interfaces Guide – Default Methods, Properties & Composition (2026)
meta.description: Master Kotlin interfaces for Android. Learn about abstract and default methods, interface properties, and how to use composition in your native modules.

content.value: Interfaces in Kotlin define a set of methods and properties that a class must implement. Unlike classes, a single Kotlin class can implement an unlimited number of interfaces. This 'Interface-based design' is critical for a Technical Lead because it decouples your high-level AI logic from the specific Android implementation details.

An interface is declared with the `interface` keyword. Unlike Java, Kotlin does not require an `implements` keyword; you simply use the colon `:` for both inheritance and interface implementation, matching the style of Swift.

One of Kotlin's strongest features is the ability to provide a default body for a method within an interface. If a class implements the interface but doesn't override the method, it inherits the default behavior. For Revochamp, this allows you to add 'Logging' or 'Analytics' traits to widgets without forcing every class to rewrite the same logic.

Interfaces can also define properties. However, these properties cannot hold state (they cannot have 'backing fields'). They are essentially a requirement for the implementing class to provide a value or a getter/setter.

When a class implements two interfaces that have the same default method, Kotlin requires the developer to explicitly override that method to resolve the ambiguity. This ensures your native Android Runner never behaves unpredictably.

faq.question: Can an interface in Kotlin have a constructor?
faq.answer: No. Interfaces cannot be instantiated directly and cannot hold state, so they do not have constructors. If you need a constructor, you must use an 'Abstract Class' instead.

faq.question: What is the difference between an Interface and an Abstract Class?
faq.answer: An Abstract Class can hold state (variables with backing fields) and can have a constructor. An Interface is purely for defining behavior (contracts) and cannot hold state, but it allows for multiple 'inheritance' which classes do not.

faq.question: Should I use an Interface or a Mixin (in Flutter context)?
faq.answer: In native Android, Interfaces with default implementations serve a very similar purpose to Mixins. Use them when you want to share a specific capability across unrelated classes (like 'Clickable' or 'Draggable').

quiz.question: Which keyword is used to define an interface in Kotlin?
True or False: Kotlin interfaces can provide a default implementation for methods.
How does a class implement multiple interfaces in Kotlin?
options: By using the 'implements' keyword multiple times
By separating interface names with commas after the colon (:)
By nesting one interface inside another
Kotlin does not support multiple interfaces
